speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to Git Going with GitHub. Before the workshop really begins, this episode is about getting everything ready so Day 1 can start with learning, not troubleshooting.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that matters a lot for this audience, right? Because setup is never just setup. It can include browser quirks, screen reader settings, account security, and a bunch of little choices that affect whether GitHub feels smooth or frustrating.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This is a Community Access workshop, and the core message is: please do this at least one day before the event. If something goes wrong, use the Community Access support repository and support discussions so help can happen before class time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I also noticed a big theme here: tools change.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. GitHub.com, GitHub Classroom, VS Code, Copilot, browsers, extensions, and agent tools all evolve. So if a label moves or a prompt sounds different from what we describe, that does not mean you did something wrong. Explore with headings, landmarks, form fields, labels, or in VS Code use the Command Palette. And when you ask for help, describe the exact page or prompt where you got stuck.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with what you need in practical terms. Hardware first: a Windows or macOS computer, a reliable internet connection, and headphones are recommended so your screen reader audio stays clear during group sessions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Software for Day 1 is pretty manageable too. You need a modern browser. Chrome or Firefox are recommended. Edge is acceptable on Windows. Safari is the recommended browser on macOS with VoiceOver.

speaker: ALEX
text: You also need one screen reader, not all of them. Use the one you already know if possible. The workshop supports NVDA on Windows, JAWS on Windows, and VoiceOver on macOS or iOS. And you need a GitHub account. Free tier is fine.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's the software you need before the workshop begins: Git, Visual Studio Code, and access to GitHub Copilot, either through a paid plan or Copilot Free.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And one version requirement is especially important: VS Code 1.113 or later, because later in the course, accessibility agents depend on newer Copilot Chat features. There are also minimums for advanced and optional tools: latest GitHub Copilot extension, Node.js 18 if you plan to use optional CLI accessibility tools, and Git 2.20 or later for workshop Git operations.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone wants to check versions, they can run code dash dash version, node dash dash version if Node is installed, and git dash dash version.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. And if Node is missing, that is okay unless you plan to do those optional Day 2 command line accessibility exercises.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we move on, can we say the screen reader choices clearly one more time?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. NVDA is free on Windows. JAWS is paid, though a trial may be available, also on Windows. VoiceOver is built into macOS and iOS and can be turned on with Command-F5 on the Mac. The workshop is designed so you are not disadvantaged by choosing one of these over another.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next is creating your GitHub account.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone already has one, they can skip ahead in their actual setup, but they still should listen here because email verification and two-factor authentication are important.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's right. If you are creating a new account, go to the GitHub signup page. The form is a single page with several fields. You'll enter your email, create a password, choose a username, answer whether you want product updates, complete a human verification step, and then activate Create account.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's slow down on the username. This is not just a technical field.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Your GitHub username appears on issues, pull requests, and comments. So choose something professional. Lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens are a good pattern. GitHub will tell you if the name is already taken.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now the hard part: the CAPTCHA.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For screen reader users, GitHub's visual CAPTCHA can be an accessibility barrier. When that challenge appears, look for an Audio option or something like Try an audio challenge. If an audio alternative is available, use it. If no accessible option appears, contact the workshop organizer before the event so they can help with account verification.

speaker: JAMIE
text: After account creation, GitHub sends a launch code to your email inbox, and you type that code back into the site. Then there may be a personalization page you can skip or answer.

speaker: ALEX
text: And do not forget the separate email verification message. GitHub sends a dedicated verify-your-email message, and some features, including creating repositories, depend on a verified email address.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then we come to two-factor authentication, or 2FA.

speaker: ALEX
text: And this is both a security issue and a workshop readiness issue. GitHub requires 2FA for accounts, so you may already have it enabled. If not, set it up now from GitHub security settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the recommended method?

speaker: ALEX
text: The preferred order is GitHub Mobile app first, because push approval is often the smoothest experience for this workshop. Other methods may include authenticator apps or other verification methods GitHub offers, but the important point is to finish setup and make sure you can actually sign in successfully afterward.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give the quick version of what to do?

speaker: ALEX
text: While signed in, open GitHub security settings, find Two-factor authentication, choose Enable, follow the prompts, pick your method, complete verification, and save what GitHub gives you for recovery.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the recovery piece is non-negotiable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Save backup or recovery codes somewhere secure. If your phone changes, is lost, or the app stops working, those codes may be the difference between quick recovery and losing access to your account.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source also includes detailed setup notes and a workshop policy around this.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The policy is simple: arrive with a working GitHub account you can sign into. Because we will be using GitHub live, account access problems can block everything else.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about signing Git in to GitHub later on? Beginners often think they need to set up SSH keys immediately.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good question. For this workshop, authenticating Git with GitHub can happen through browser-based sign-in using OAuth when prompted by VS Code or GitHub-related tools. That is often the most approachable path for beginners. SSH keys and personal access tokens are reference topics in the Git authentication appendix, not the required starting point here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Once the account is ready, the next job is making GitHub easier to use with assistive tech.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we're going into GitHub accessibility settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Navigate to your settings while signed in and find the accessibility area. There are four settings to pay attention to.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The highest priority one is disabling hovercards.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Hovercards can interrupt screen reader flow and clutter navigation with extra dynamic content. Turning them off usually makes GitHub calmer and more predictable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next, enable link underlines.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. That helps low-vision users and anyone who benefits from stronger visual distinction between plain text and links.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there are character key shortcuts.

speaker: ALEX
text: Those are single-key shortcuts on GitHub. Some users like them. Others find they interfere with screen reader or browser commands. The key idea is to review that setting deliberately rather than leaving it unexplored.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then theme appearance settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Choose a theme that gives you comfortable contrast and readability. That might mean dark, light, dim, or another appearance option depending on your visual needs.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this step is not cosmetic. It's about reducing friction before the real work begins.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's profile setup, which some people might think is optional.

speaker: ALEX
text: But it matters socially and practically. In open source, maintainers are the people who review contributions, guide project direction, and help keep a project healthy. When they look at a contributor's profile, they are often just trying to understand who is participating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So updating your profile helps you appear as a real person, not just a mysterious username.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's right. Add your name if you want, a short bio, maybe location or links if relevant. A profile picture is strongly recommended because it makes comments and pull requests easier for others to recognize.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the notification email setting is important too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very. Make sure the email GitHub uses for notifications is one you actually check. Otherwise you may miss replies, review requests, issue comments, and classroom-related updates.

speaker: ALEX
text: After that, check GitHub feature preview settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This part can sound mysterious. What is a feature preview in plain language?

speaker: ALEX
text: It's GitHub's way of letting some users opt into newer interface features before they become standard. Depending on your account, there may be previews available, or there may be none listed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if it says Not Listed?

speaker: ALEX
text: That usually just means there are no available previews for your account or region at that moment. It does not mean your account is broken or missing something required.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a note specifically for NVDA or JAWS users on Windows.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you're checking these settings with NVDA or JAWS, use your usual form field and heading navigation, and be prepared for labels or arrangement to vary. Again, tool updates happen.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what do these feature previews enable?

speaker: ALEX
text: They may unlock newer experiences or interface changes GitHub is testing. The big lesson here is not to chase every preview, but to know where to look and understand that availability varies.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's tune the screen reader and browser themselves.

speaker: ALEX
text: For NVDA on Windows, use a browser mode setup that supports comfortable heading, landmark, link, and form navigation on complex web pages. The guide also recommends adjusting voice settings so speech is clear and efficient during GitHub workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Not too slow, not too fast, and probably enough punctuation or formatting cues to make code and interface labels understandable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. For JAWS on Windows, similarly configure it for efficient web browsing and review any settings that improve interaction with dynamic GitHub pages. Recommended JAWS settings for GitHub focus on reducing confusion and improving consistency when navigating dense interfaces.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for VoiceOver on macOS?

speaker: ALEX
text: There are a few essentials. Make sure VoiceOver is set up for web navigation in a way you're comfortable with. Turn on Quick Nav if that helps you move faster with arrow keys. And there's an important note for Mac users: keyboard shortcuts can differ because macOS, VoiceOver, browsers, and VS Code all have their own command layers. So when a shortcut behaves differently, that may be a platform difference, not a mistake.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we summarize browser recommendations cleanly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Chrome or Firefox are strong choices generally. Edge is fine on Windows. Safari is the preferred browser with VoiceOver on macOS. The best browser is the one that works reliably with your screen reader and lets you navigate GitHub with the least friction.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next, install Git and Visual Studio Code.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Git first.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, install Git before anything else. On Windows, download Git from git-scm.com and run the installer. On macOS, Git commonly comes through Xcode Command Line Tools. If macOS prompts you to install command line tools when you first use Git, follow that prompt.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And verification matters here too.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. On Windows, verify installation by opening a terminal and running git dash dash version. You should hear or see a version number. There's also a screen reader note here: terminal output can be easy to miss, so review carefully, use reading commands, and confirm you actually heard the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then install VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Download and install Visual Studio Code from the official site. Once it opens, immediately turn on Screen Reader Mode.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There are two ways to do that.

speaker: ALEX
text: Option A is the keyboard shortcut if available in your environment. Option B is more universal: open the Command Palette, search for Screen Reader Mode, and enable it there.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And how do you confirm it's on?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can check the setting through the interface or Command Palette and verify that VS Code is presenting information in a more screen-reader-friendly way. The important concept is what Screen Reader Mode changes: it adjusts editor and interface behavior to be more compatible with assistive technology, including how content is announced and navigated.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The guide also mentions VS Code keyboard basics, though those will be taught more fully later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For now, just know that keyboard-first work in VS Code is expected, and the workshop will teach those skills in context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: After the tools are installed, we configure Git identity.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where Git records your name and email on commits. In VS Code, you can open the integrated terminal and configure Git with your user name and user email.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people use for those values?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the name you want attached to your commits, usually your real name or the consistent professional name you use in GitHub spaces. For email, use the correct GitHub-associated email. Ideally it matches the verified email you intend GitHub to recognize for commit attribution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why does this matter so much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because commits are historical records. If your identity is wrong, your work may not be properly attributed to your GitHub account, and your commit history can become confusing to you and to collaborators.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So verification is part of the step, not an optional extra.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Run Git config commands to inspect the values you set and make sure name and email are correct. And be especially careful about the email. Using the wrong email is one of the most common reasons commits fail to connect cleanly with a GitHub profile.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then we install the VS Code extensions needed for the workshop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: First, GitHub Copilot is built in.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Modern VS Code includes GitHub Copilot support automatically. You may still need to activate it by signing in with your GitHub account and completing any prompts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there's the GitHub Pull Requests extension.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Install the GitHub Pull Requests extension in VS Code. After installation, verify it's working by confirming that GitHub-related panels, commands, or sign-in integration become available.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a note about Copilot Free.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Learners do not need a paid Copilot plan to participate. Copilot Free is available to GitHub users, and that is enough for the workshop path described here.

speaker: JAMIE
text: At this point, we've done a lot. So let's talk verification checklist.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. Before Day 1, confirm all the basics: you can sign into GitHub, your email is verified, 2FA works, your accessibility settings are adjusted, your profile is updated, your screen reader and browser feel comfortable, Git is installed, VS Code is installed with Screen Reader Mode enabled, Git identity is configured, and the required VS Code integrations are available.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the tool-card mindset: don't assume. Check.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And at the start of Day 1, the workshop will move quickly into the actual learning environment. So if setup is complete beforehand, you can focus on GitHub Classroom, repositories, editing, Git workflows, and contribution practices instead of scrambling through installation.

speaker: ALEX
text: There are also a few access methods mentioned for reference only, not as workshop requirements.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Those are GitHub Desktop and the GitHub CLI, usually called gh.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Both are valid tools in the wider GitHub ecosystem, but they are not the main path for this workshop. There's also a reference mention of GitHub Copilot CLI, gh copilot, and of Git itself as the underlying version control system. Useful to know they exist, but not part of the required setup flow here.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That distinction matters, especially for beginners who can get overwhelmed and think they need every tool.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You do not need every tool. You need the right baseline tools.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if somebody still gets stuck?

speaker: ALEX
text: They should not wait quietly. Use the support options before the event. Open a support issue in the Community Access support repository, join the support hub discussions and read the pinned Start Here guidance, or visit the GitHub Community accessibility discussions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like the tone of that section. You will not be left behind.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Every setup problem solved before Day 1 creates more room for real learning during the workshop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, can we mention where the current source of truth lives, since tools and interfaces change?

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. When in doubt, consult official GitHub Docs, the GitHub Changelog, and core GitHub documentation on Git, GitHub flow, pull requests, issues, and contributing to projects. Those are the authoritative references behind this setup guidance.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the goal of this pre-work episode is simple: arrive ready, secure, and comfortable in your tools.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Have your account working, your accessibility settings tuned, your browser and screen reader cooperating, Git and VS Code installed, your Git identity configured, and your extensions ready. Then move on to the next lesson knowing your foundation is solid.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's it for this setup conversation.

speaker: ALEX
text: And once you're ready, continue to the next part of the course and start choosing your tools with confidence.
